content: Sleep disorder diagnosis requires clinical evaluation and often sleep studies. OSA: in-lab polysomnography (PSG) is gold standard—measures EEG, EOG, EMG, airflow, respiratory effort, oxygen saturation, ECG, leg movements. AHI = (apneas + hypopneas)/hour sleep. Home sleep apnea testing (HSAT) acceptable for uncomplicated suspected OSA. Narcolepsy: overnight PSG followed by Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT)—mean sleep latency <8 minutes plus ≥2 sleep-onset REM periods (SOREMPs) confirms diagnosis. CSF hypocretin-1 low in narcolepsy type 1. RLS: clinical diagnosis using four essential criteria (urge to move legs, worse at rest, relieved by movement, worse evening/night); supported by periodic limb movements on PSG, low ferritin. Insomnia: clinical diagnosis based on history; PSG not routinely indicated unless suspect sleep apnea, periodic limb movement disorder, or parasomnias. Sleep diaries and actigraphy (wearable movement monitor) for 1-2 weeks assess sleep patterns and circadian rhythms. Epworth Sleepiness Scale quantifies daytime sleepiness (score >10 abnormal). Differential diagnosis: insufficient sleep syndrome, depression, medication effects, medical conditions (pain, nocturia, dyspnea).
